explanation_md: Extracting `abc` into an `int` fails, setting `failbit`. The condition is false, so the `else` branch runs. Because extraction failed, `n` retains its initialised value of `99`. Output is `fail 99`.

prompt_md: After `std::cin.clear()` is called, why is it still necessary to call `std::cin.ignore(...)`?

options.a: `clear()` only resets `badbit`; `ignore()` is needed to reset `failbit`.
options.b: `clear()` resets the error flags but leaves the invalid characters in the buffer; `ignore()` discards them.
options.c: `clear()` flushes the buffer; `ignore()` re-fills it from the OS.
options.d: `clear()` is deprecated; `ignore()` handles both flag clearing and buffer cleanup.

explanation_md: `clear()` resets the error flags so the stream is willing to read again, but the characters that caused the failure (e.g. `abc`) are still sitting in the input buffer. The next read will hit them again and fail immediately. `ignore()` discards those characters, leaving the buffer in a clean state.

`while (std::cin >> x)` reads integers until EOF or a bad read. The three integers 5, 7, and 3 are read and summed. At EOF the extraction returns a stream in a false state and the loop exits. `sum` is 15.

prompt_md: `fail()` returns true when which flag(s) are set?
explanation_md: `fail()` returns true if `failbit` OR `badbit` is set, because a hardware-level error (`badbit`) also makes the stream unusable for logical operations. `eofbit` alone does not cause `fail()` to return true â€” reaching end of file is not itself a failure.

options.a: `std::cin.eof()` is not a valid function; use `std::feof(stdin)` instead.
options.b: Checking `!eof()` does not stop the loop when `failbit` is set by bad input; the loop spins forever re-reading the same bad data.
options.c: `sum` overflows because the loop never terminates.
options.d: `x` is uninitialized, causing undefined behaviour before the first read.

explanation_md: When non-integer input is encountered, `failbit` is set but `eofbit` is not, so `!std::cin.eof()` remains true and the loop spins. Each iteration re-reads the same offending characters, failing again. The fix is `while (std::cin >> x)` â€” the stream converts to `false` on either EOF or a failed read.
